channel.title: Logs & Events on materialize-monitoring Documentation
channel.description: Recent content in Logs & Events on materialize-monitoring Documentation

item.title: Collecting
item.description:
Collecting Logs & Events#

Logs and Kubernetes events enter Loki through its write path after Alloy collects and processes them. This page covers what the agent and gateway do, how to send your own logs in, and the knobs you can tune. See the logging architecture for how collection fits into the full pipeline.

What the agent collects#

The alloy-agent runs as a DaemonSet — one pod per node — and gathers two things from each node:

item.title: Storing
item.description:
Storing Logs#

Loki keeps all durable log data in a single object storage backend, maintained by the Loki Compactor. This page covers the storage layout, the index, retention, and disaster recovery. See the logging architecture for how storage relates to the ingesters that write it and the queriers that read it.

Object storage#

Loki is a black box over an S3-style object store. The supported backends are S3-compatible storage (AWS S3, MinIO, Ceph, R2, …), Google Cloud Storage, and Azure Blob Storage. For integration testing, Loki instead uses a local filesystem store in single-binary mode — no object storage required.

item.title: Querying
item.description:
Querying Logs#

Queries — almost always issued through Grafana — are served by Loki’s read path. This page covers how to query logs, how the read path serves them, and how to keep queries fast. See the logging architecture for how the read path fits together.

Querying through Grafana#

In the bundled stack, Grafana ships with a Loki datasource pre-provisioned by the Grafana operator, so logs are queryable from Explore and from dashboard panels without extra setup. Queries are written in LogQL, Loki’s query language:

item.title: Rules
item.description:
Log and Event Rules#

Alerting and recording rules over logs are evaluated by the Loki Ruler on a schedule. This page covers the two rule kinds, where their output goes, and how rules are stored and scaled. See the logging architecture for where the ruler sits in the pipeline.

Two kinds of rule#

flowchart LR
 logs[("Stored logs")] --> ruler["Loki Ruler"]
 ruler -->|"alerting rules → alerts"| am["Alertmanager"]
 ruler -->|"recording rules → samples<br/>remote_write"| gw["alloy-gateway"]
 gw -->|"prometheus remote_write"| thanos[("Long-term metric store<br/>Thanos")]

Alerting rules#

An alerting rule is a LogQL expression that, when it crosses a threshold for a configured duration, fires an alert. The ruler sends fired alerts to Alertmanager, which groups, routes, and notifies through the configured channels. This is how log-based conditions — a spike in ERROR lines, a specific failure pattern — page the on-call alongside metric-based alerts.
